justification_text:
The point estimates I provided for each time horizon—one day, one week, and one month—are based on expected trends and patterns in the data, incorporating recent observations, historical behaviors, and any forward-looking indicators. For the one-day forecast, the point estimate is closer to the most recent data since short-term changes are typically small and more predictable in many systems. For the one-week and one-month forecasts, the estimates gradually incorporate broader trends or mean reversion effects, as well as evolving conditions over longer periods. These horizons, therefore, reflect a greater degree of projected movement relative to the starting point, though not excessively since major shifts generally take time.

The width of the confidence intervals at each horizon reflects the uncertainty inherent in forecasting, which grows with time. For the one-day forecast, uncertainties are lower because short-term predictions tend to show less variability unless sudden shocks occur. However, as the horizon lengthens to one week and one month, uncertainty around the projection increases, partly due to the compounding effects of small deviations over time, as well as the influence of new or unpredictable factors. The broader interval for the one-month forecast also accounts for long-term risks, structural changes, or events that may not be apparent in the current data.

While the confidence intervals aim to capture most reasonable outcomes, there are always risks that could push results outside of those ranges. In this context, sudden or extreme external events—like a market shock, geopolitical developments, or major economic data releases—could spark unexpected volatility. Additionally, systemic shocks (e.g., policy changes or technological disruptions) could break established patterns entirely. These types of risks are difficult to quantify precisely but are part of why longer-horizon forecasts naturally carry wider intervals.
